title: Total Nucleic Acids Extraction from Soil
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"6ev2n","text":"The following protocol is intended for the simultaneous extraction of DNA and RNA (total nucleic acids, or TNA) from various soil and sediment samples. The protocol was designed based on two protocols published by Henckel et al. (1999) and Griffiths et al. (2000), with several critical modifications. Recently, we have added the option to include an ammonium aluminium sulfate salt for soils with high humic content (Braid et al., 2003). The result is a highly flexible and streamlined protocol, which delivers high yields of nucleic acids with quality suitable for all downstream molecular applications from most types of soil and sediment samples. Please cite Angel et al. (2012).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":214,"length":21},{"key":1,"offset":240,"length":23},{"key":2,"offset":418,"length":18},{"key":3,"offset":663,"length":19}],"data":{}},{"key":"42smv","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e66gc","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"f7eiu","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4b8r8","text":" guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"if74","text":"RNA handling. Since RNA is very sensitive to both chemical and enzymatic degradation, some precautionary measures should be taken. Commonly, DEPC treated water is used in RNA extraction protocols; however, please note that the substance is itself toxic and tends to break down to methanol when heated (e.g. by autoclaving). RNase free water (DEPC treated or not) can be purchased and is rather inexpensive. In addition, it is recommended to work with clean, preferably baked, glassware (3 h at 180 °C) for storing tubes, to clean surfaces, spatulas, pipettes and centrifuge parts with RNase eliminating solutions (such as RNase AWAY™), and to use fresh and clean reagents for the preparation of the various buffers and solutions (keep salt stocks separate from general chemical storage or label them as “RNA only” to avoid contamination). Do not autoclave tips or tubes; this is generally unnecessary for molecular work and might get them dirty or compromise the material. Instead, use tubes and tips marked as DNase/RNase free. Filter tips are preferred over standard tips. As a general rule, work with RNA should be quick, precise, and samples should be kept on ice, when possible. In our experience, this is much more important than any attempt to eliminate all RNases from apparatus and solutions. One should also bear in mind that soil samples contain far more RNases than any contamination that might exist in the buffers or the apparatus. Therefore, one should not worry too much about the purity of the extraction buffer and phosphate buffer. In contrast, the PEG Precipitation Solution, the Low TE Buffer, and the RNA storage solution are used to store extracted RNA for relatively long periods of incubation. Hence, extra care must be taken during their preparation to avoid contamination. When preparing the PEG Precipitation Solution, it is better to err on the side of precise concentration rather than risk contamination. To minimise the handling during this step (i.e. avoid transferring liquids between different flasks), simply weigh everything into a 250 ml DURAN Glass Bottle and slowly add water until the liquid reaches the 200 ml mark (after all solids have been soaked in the water), then shake the bottle vigorously and autoclave it. Prepare Low TE Buffer from pre-sold Tris and EDTA solutions (see Step 1.4).","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":14}],"entityRanges":[{"key":0,"offset":2323,"length":8}],"data":[]},{"key":"6fokn","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6tl07","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":1,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"2noo","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ber52","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":2,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"eogcs","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"6qnco","text":"Choice of extraction buffer. Using TNS buffer results in higher DNA and RNA yields and more intact rRNA. However, TNS tends to carry over significantly more humic substances with the nucleic acids compared to TNC buffer. We, therefore, recommend using TNS only for mineral soil and sediment samples (see Step 1.2).","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":29}],"entityRanges":[{"key":3,"offset":304,"length":8}],"data":[]},{"key":"40iq3","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"die7d","text":"Reducing the carryover of humic substances. Adding aluminium salt in the form of AlNH4(SO4)2*12H2O to the phosphate buffer has been shown to decrease the concentration of humic substances in the extract and is recommended for very organic soils (see Step 1.1). For more detail see Braid et al., (2003). ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":44},{"style":"sub","offset":89,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":91,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":96,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[{"key":4,"offset":250,"length":8},{"key":5,"offset":281,"length":20}],"data":[]},{"key":"dscfg","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":6,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"4uq1c","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4hj1d","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":7,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"8rhs9","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"b406t","text":"Storing and working with phenol and chloroform. Store phenol in a cool, dry, ventilated area away from sources of heat or ignition. Store separately from reactive or combustible materials and out of direct sunlight. Phenol will begin to oxidise once opened and should be used within a few weeks to a few months, depending on storage conditions and temperature. Older, oxidised, phenol solutions should not be used as they may cause “nicking” of the DNA. The phenol solution should contain an anti-oxidising agent (8-hydroxyquinoline) as an indicator (i.e., if the 8-hydroxyquinoline is oxidised, the phenol solution will turn a reddish colour).","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":48}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9ua67","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":8,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"d9vfg","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"2ukd1","text":"Use of phenol in the bead beating process. Using phenol increases the yield up to 4 times compared with the phenol-free option (unpublished data), but also increases somewhat the carryover of humic substances. Still, considering the increased yield we recommend using phenol in the bead beating steps for all but the trickiest samples. If omitting phenol, double the volume of the extraction buffer and phosphate buffers added (to 0.9 ml in total).","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":43}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"a5gbh","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ak3pi","text":"Homogenizing the sample at a lower temperature.  MP Biomedicals offers an adaptor to their FastPrep homogeniser that allows sample homogenising at a lower temperature using dry ice. We would highly recommend using this adaptor, in particular, when processing samples in the presence of phenol (see above). From our experience, processing samples at a low temperature significantly reduces carryover of humic acids, helps to protect RNA from degradation and also the tubes from accidental overheating and leakage of phenol. Use only one or maximum two tablespoons full of dry Ice because overloading the adapter with dry ice (especially when not using phenol) could cause the sample and buffer to freeze in during the bead beating process! ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":47},{"style":"bold","offset":522,"length":217}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"65sm","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":9,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"6bnki","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"egb68","text":"LifeGuard Soil Preservation Solution. If samples were stored in an RNA-preservation solution such as LifeGuard Soil Preservation Solution it is necessary to remove it before proceeding with RNA extraction. Centrifuge the sample at 10,000 rpm for 1 min and pipette the supernatant. Add 1 ml of PB (see Step 1.1), vortex the sample for 10 s to make sure the soil is washed by the buffer, and centrifuge it again at 10,000 rpm for 1 min. Remove the supernatant completely and proceed with normal extraction. ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":37}],"entityRanges":[{"key":10,"offset":301,"length":8}],"data":[]},{"key":"aaakf","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":11,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"5c9g9","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1mk8r","text":"Very dry soil samples. For very dry soil (\u003c10% WC) it is recommended to add some (up to 250 μl) PB pH 8.0 before starting the extraction (before the first bead beating processing). The reason is that in dry soils, some of the extraction buffer solution gets absorbed to the soil and cannot be recovered afterwards. This can lead to low recovery volumes from the bead-beating process, which could make it hard to separate the aqueous phase from the phenolic phase in the subsequent steps and eventually result in a reduced yield. After the first round of bead-beating, the soil is wet enough, and no further additions are required.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":22}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"c689n","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"f0rd6","text":"pH of the extraction buffers. The pH of the phenol and the extraction buffers used in this protocol is set to 8.0. It has been shown that performing the extraction at around pH 5.0 significantly reduces the carryover of humic substances and is recommended for soils rich in organic material. Using such low pH, however, will also considerably decrease the amount of DNA yield (while supposedly not affecting RNA yield). Low pH extraction might therefore not be suitable if DNA is also to be analysed (particularly in a quantitative way). If opting for this option use the PB pH 5.8 in combination with water-saturated phenol. For more information see Mettel et al., (2010). ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":29}],"entityRanges":[{"key":12,"offset":651,"length":21}],"data":[]},{"key":"8n3e7","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":13,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"3aosf","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9qm84","text":"Number of bead beating processing repeats. For most soil types (especially if they’re not too clayish), single bead-beating processing should be sufficient to obtain enough NA for further applications. The following two repetitions in this protocol (see Step 8) is meant not only to increase the yield but also to reduce the bias associated with the susceptibility of different cell types to lysis. Feinstein et al., (2009) found that DNA extraction yields kept increasing even after the 6th bead beating repetition, and that community structure differed somewhat in each repetition.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":42}],"entityRanges":[{"key":14,"offset":254,"length":6},{"key":15,"offset":399,"length":24}],"data":[]},{"key":"fjopu","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":16,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"822ql","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"59hm3","text":"Amount of crude extract to use for RNA purification. This depends mostly on the colour of the crude extract, and its quantity and purity as measured spectrophotometrically. In addition, the decision should take into account the final volume of the RNA. Between 20-100 μl of total extract may be used depending on colour, purity and amount of NA.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":52}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"26fsu","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9g74r","text":"Quantification.  It is not possible to precisely quantify total NA using a spectrophotometer (e.g. Nanodrop) since DNA and RNA require different multiplication factors and since the presence of co-extracted organic compounds from soil (humic substances) often obscure the measurement. More precise quantification of DNA and RNA can be obtained using specific fluorescent dyes (e.g. Invitrogen’s PicoGreen™ and RiboGreen™). For more details see DNA quantification with PicoGreen and RNA quantification with RiboGreen. Quantification of a TNA solution using PicoGreen should only give the amount of DNA while using RiboGreen should yield twice the amount of DNA plus that of RNA. Thus, it is possible to quantify both DNA and RNA in a TNA solution without having to separate the two.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":16}],"entityRanges":[{"key":17,"offset":444,"length":33},{"key":18,"offset":482,"length":33}],"data":[]},{"key":"4428n","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"efv37","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":19,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"fp65e","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"1jsbn","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"4dcmm","text":" ","type":"atomic","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[{"key":20,"offset":0,"length":1}],"data":[]},{"key":"6ekcb","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"f86bh","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"anbon","text":"Triple purification procedure. Dispose of the wipes in a fume hood. If a spill occurs outside the hood. Larger spills should be handled by professionals.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"ejdrq","text":"In the event of skin contact, immediately remove contaminated clothing and wipe the area using an LMW-PEG-soaked absorbent pad until no phenol smell is noticeable. When LMW PEG is not available, flushing the exposed area with copious amounts of water for 15 minutes may be effective. In case of contact with eyes, immediately flush eyes with copious amounts of water for at least 15 minutes and subsequently obtain medical attention. All exposed persons should be removed from the area and seek immediate medical attention (subsequent to initial decontamination for skin/eye contact). In the event of ingestion, obtain immediate medical attention. Do not induce vomiting unless","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"92k7u","text":"directed to do so by medical personnel.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"b0rh1","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"dcqjl","text":"First Aid Kit","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":5},{"style":"bold","offset":6,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"sl8o","text":"All labs utilizing phenol shall keep a first aid kit on hand containing:","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":72}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"cmrf","text":"At least one-litre pharmaceutical grade polyethylene glycol (PEG), 300 or 400 molecular mass, e.g. Kollisolv® PEG 300 or 400 (Sigma Aldrich 91462-1KG or 06855-1KG). PEG 300/400 is a skin-safe, excellent phenol solvent.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9qeil","text":"Laminate film gloves (Barrier®, Silver Shield®) for use by colleagues who are helping with decontamination. Do not put gloves on if your hand is already contaminated!","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"po3l","text":"Large cotton roll (e.g. VWR 470161-446).","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"eq81o","text":"Wiping cloths (e.g. VWR 500030-610 or 500030-611).","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"45q3b","text":"Selection of thick polyethylene bags for holding contaminated waste (e.g. large Ziploc® storage bags).","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"7252t","text":"Large squeeze-bottle of liquid hand soap (A squeeze bottle allows for faster application to the body than a pump dispenser).","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"8q03d","text":"Copy of this SOP with the first aid section highlighted and copy of a phenol SDS from a reputable supplier (e.g. Sigma Aldrich).","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"b5g12","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"9243h","text":"Storage and waste","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":0,"length":17}],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"3uvq2","text":"Phenol is a combustible acid. It must be stored so that it cannot come in contact with strong oxidizers (such as nitric acid and bromine) and strong bases (such as potassium hydroxide) because a violent reaction could result. ","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"97q9q","text":"Store below eye level to prevent injuries in case of a spill.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"a6kus","text":"Phenol waste should be placed in a container that is clearly labelled and has a securely sealed lid.","type":"unordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]},{"key":"fdmke","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":[]}],"entityMap":{"0":{"type":"safety","mutability":"","data":{"blocks":[{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[{"length":34,"offset":0,"style":"bold"}],"key":"cr5r8","text":"Working with phenol and chloroform","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[{"length":6,"offset":0,"style":"bold"},{"length":1,"offset":915,"style":"sup"}],"key":"d9864","text":"Phenol is highly toxic for humans and animals and is also classified as mutagenic, teratogenic and potentially carcinogenic. Phenol is readily absorbed through intact skin and is highly toxic to cells. Cellular damage and death at the site of entry results in a chemical burn, which may be extremely serious. There can be a time delay between absorption of phenol, and the appearance of burn symptoms and phenol is a local anaesthetic which numbs sensory nerve endings; for both these reasons, phenol contamination may not be noticed until considerable absorption and damage has occurred. Phenol-chloroform may have even faster-penetrating ability than phenol alone, particularly towards glove materials. Besides the local toxic effect leading to burns, phenol also exerts systemic toxic effects on humans, which may lead to rapid poisoning if sufficient phenol is absorbed. Any exposure covering more than a few cm2 of skin is potentially fatal and must be considered as a medical emergency.","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"7djls","text":"","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[{"length":10,"offset":0,"style":"bold"}],"key":"cr89i","text":"Chloroform is toxic if inhaled or swallowed, can cause skin and eye irritation and is a suspected carcinogen.","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"9m59c","text":"","type":"unstyled"},{"data":[],"depth":0,"entityRanges":[],"inlineStyleRanges":[],"key":"1762c","text":"Work with phenol and chloroform should only be done inside a fume hood. Always refer to the accompanying MSDS before working with any hazardous substance. Always wear gloves, eye protection and a lab coat when working with phenol and chloroform. Latex gloves should not be used with phenol. Disposable nitrile gloves are rapidly penetrated by phenol, but they can be used provided they are exchanged for fresh gloves immediately upon becoming contaminated. Double-gloving is recommended.","type":"unstyled"}],"entityMap":{}}}}}

steps: Prepare the following solutions for TNA extraction Use clean and preferably baked glassware (make sure all non-glass components can withstand the high temperatures).

One of the following phosphate buffers: Phosphate buffer ( 120 millimolar (mM) , pH 8.0 ) undetermined, K2HPO4•3 H2O (M.W. 228.22) undetermined, KH2PO4 (M.W. 136.09) undetermined, RNase-free water Dissolve the salts in RNase-free water and fill up to 500 ml. Autoclave. Phosphate buffer ( 120 millimolar (mM) , pH 5.8 ) undetermined, K2HPO4•3 H2O (M.W. 136.09) undetermined, KH2PO4 (M.W. 136.09) undetermined, RNase-free water Dissolve the salts in RNase-free water and fill up to 500 ml. Autoclave. Phosphate buffer + AlNH 4 (120 mM PB pH 8.0 + 0.1M AlNH 4 (SO 4 ) 2 • 12H 2 O) 2.04 g, AlNH4(SO4)2 · 12H2O (M.W. 453.33) 45 mL, 120 mM PB pH 8.0 Dissolve in PB. Autoclave. Store at Room temperature Potassium phosphate dibasic trihydrate Merck MilliporeSigma (Sigma-Aldrich) Catalog #P9666 (CAS: 16788-57-1) Potassium phosphate monobasic Merck MilliporeSigma (Sigma-Aldrich) Catalog #P9791 (CAS: 7778-77-0) Nuclease-free autoclaved DEPC-treated water Carl Roth Catalog #T143.1 (CAS: 7732-18-5) Aluminium ammonium sulfate dodecahydrate Merck MilliporeSigma (Sigma-Aldrich) Catalog #1010310500 (CAS: 7784-26-1)

Either TNC or TNS TNC undetermined, TRIZMA (M.W. 121.14) undetermined, NaCl undetermined, CTAB (M.W 364.45) undetermined, RNase-free water Dissolve the salts in RNase-free water and fill up to 200 ml. Autoclave. TNS undetermined, TRIZMA (M.W 121.14) undetermined, NaCl undetermined, SDS (M.W 288.38) undetermined, RNase-free water Dissolve the salts in RNase-free water and fill up to 200 ml. Autoclave. Store at Room temperature Trizma® base Merck MilliporeSigma (Sigma-Aldrich) Catalog #93362 (CAS: 77-86-1) Sodium chloride for molecular biology Merck MilliporeSigma (Sigma-Aldrich) Catalog #S3014 Hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide Merck MilliporeSigma (Sigma-Aldrich) Catalog #H6269 Sodium dodecyl sulfate Merck MilliporeSigma (Sigma-Aldrich) Catalog #3771

PEG precipitation solution (30%) undetermined, PEG (M.W 7000-9000) undetermined, NaCl undetermined, RNase-free water Add ingredients to a graduated Duran bottle. Add water to fill up to 200 ml, shake vigorously by hand, autoclave and mix well while hot (solution turns milky when hot, but then turns clear when cooled to room temperature). Store at Room temperature Polyethylene glycol 8000 Carl Roth Catalog #0263.1
Molecular-grade ethanol solution (75%) 75 mL, Absolute ethanol 25 mL, RNase-free water Store at -20 °C Ethanol, Absolute, Molecular Biology Grade Thermo Fisher Scientific Catalog #BP2818500

Low-EDTA TE buffer undetermined, Tris-HCl 1 M, pH 8.0 (Trizma) undetermined, EDTA 0.5 M, pH 8.0 undetermined, RNase-free water Prepare in a laminar-flow hood (to protect stocks), filter sterilise (0.2 µm) and autoclave. Store at Room temperature UltraPure™ 1M Tris-HCI pH 8.0 Thermo Fisher Scientific Catalog #15568025 UltraPure™ 0.5M EDTA pH 8.0 Thermo Fisher Scientific Catalog #15575020

Weigh 0.4 g, soil (0.2-0.7 g) into a Lysing Matrix E tube, or a 2-ml screw-cap tube filled with 0.7 g (one full PCR tube) of 0.1 mm glass beads and place the tube On ice . Lysing Matrix E 2 mL tube MP Biomedicals Catalog #116914050-CF

Add 375 µL of either PB pH 8.0, PB pH 5.8 or PB+Al, 125 µL of TNC or TNS and 400 µL of TE-saturated phenol (if using PB pH 8.0) or water-saturated phenol (if using PB pH 5.8). Alternatively, add 675 µL of PB, 225 µL of TNC or TNS, if not using phenol (see Guidelines and Warnings on which combination of solutions to choose.) ROTI® Phenol Carl Roth Catalog #0038.1 (CAS: 108-95-2) ROTI® Aqua-Phenol Carl Roth Catalog #A980.1 (CAS: 108-95-2) Lysing Matrix E tubes with soil and reagents prepared for the 1st bead beating round.

Immediately place the tube in a sample homogeniser and process for 00:00:30 at 6.5 m s -1 . We recommend using the FastPrep-24™ sample homogenizer with the CoolPrep™ adapter for 24 x 2 ml On ice, (dry ice) . If using the CoolPrep™ adapter make sure all slots are filled with either sample or empty tubes. The bottom part of a CoolPrep FastPrep adaptor filled with the recommended amount of dry ice. FastPrep-24™ 5G bead beating grinder and lysis system (Sample homogenizer, MP Biomedicals, 116005500, https://www.mpbio.com/au/fastprep-24-5g-instrument ) CoolPrep™ adapter for 24 x 2 mL tube holder on FastPrep-24 (Sample homogeniser adapter, MP Biomedicals, 116002528, https://www.mpbio.com/au/catalogsearch/result/?q=coolprep ) Dry Ice Contributed by users FastPrep-24™ 5G set up. The green-capped tubes are empty and are used to seal the CoolPrep adapter. The samples right after bead beating.
Chill On ice for 00:00:10 ( not necessary if using the CoolPrep™ adapter ).
Centrifuge at 14000 rpm, 15 °C, 3 minutes (centrifugation at RT is also possible). The samples after centrifugation.

Transfer the entire liquid (aqueous and organic phases) to a fresh 2 ml tube by decanting or pipetting. tubes. Your supernatant has two phases. An aqueous upper phase that should be transparent/translucent and a phenol phase that should be opaque. Make sure to pour both into the fresh 2 ml tube. Tubes set up for 8 samples: A) Lysing Matrix E in a green stand (1 tube per sample). B) 2 ml tubes for collecting the supernatant after processing in a yellow stand (2 tubes per sample). The collected supernatant at this stage.

Repeat Steps 3-7 two more times using the same Lysing Matrix E tube. Be careful not to overfill the tube as this might cause phenol leakage during the sample homogenising process. If there is not enough space in the tube for all the reagents, decrease the phenol volume (down to 200 μl). For the second homogenising repetition use a fresh 2 ml tube to collect the supernatant in Step 7 , while for the third repetition divide the supernatant between the two tubes from the previous repetitions to achieve equal volumes. go to step #3

Add 800 µL, phenol/chloroform/isoamyl alcohol 25:24:1 (or 1 volume) to each of the tubes containing the supernatant, to a maximum of 2 ml total volume in each tube. ROTI® Phenol/ Chloroform/ Isoamyl alcohol Carl Roth Catalog #A156.1 (CAS: 136112-00-0)
Mix the two phases , by hand or using a vortex. Centrifuge 14000 rpm, 15 °C, 3 minutes . Following centrifugation, the liquid in each tube should separate into two phases.

Using a 1-ml pipette tip, carefully transfer the aqueous phase (the upper phase) from each tube to two fresh 2 ml tubes. Be careful not to touch or pipette the interphase or the organic phase (the lower phase) If, by mistake, some of the interphase or organic phase were pipetted, simply return the liquid to the original tube and centrifuge it again.
Add undetermined, chloroform/isoamyl alcohol 24:1 (or 1 volume) to each tube. ROTI® C/I Carl Roth Catalog #X984.1
Mix the phases vigorously by hand or using a vortex. Centrifuge 14000 rpm, 15 °C, 3 minutes . Following centrifugation, the liquid in each tube should separate into two phases.

Carefully transfer the supernatant from each tube to a fresh 2 ml low-binding microcentrifuge tubes. At this point, you should have two low-binding tubes per sample. If, by mistake, some of the interphase or organic phase were pipetted, simply return the liquid to the original tube and centrifuge it again. DNA LoBind Tubes (Microcentrifuge tubes, Eppendorf, 0030108051, https://online-shop.eppendorf.com/OC-en/Laboratory-Consumables-44512/Tubes-44515/DNA-LoBind-Tubes-PF-56252.html )
Add to each tube 2 µL, RNA-grade glycogen and 1 mL, PEG Precipitation Solution (or twice the extract’s volume). Glycogen RNA grade Thermo Fisher Scientific Catalog #R0551 (CAS: 9005-79-2)

Centrifuge 14000 rpm, 4 °C, 1 hour . A pellet should be visible at the bottom of the LoBind tube after centrifugation. The pellet should be white/opaque in colour. The size of the pellet will depend on the TNA content in a sample, but also on the amount of co-extracted contaminants.

Decant the supernatant, briefly centrifuge or spin-down again to collect the drops and using a pipette remove as much as possible from the remaining precipitation solution. Be careful not to disturb the pellet.
Add 1 mL, ice-cold 75% EtOH , invert the tube several times. Centrifuge at 14000 rpm, 4 °C, 20 minutes .
Remove the supernatant using a pipette, shortly centrifuge again to collect the drops and using a pipette remove as much as possible from the remaining ethanol. Be careful not to disturb the pellet.

Leave the tubes open at Room temperature for approximately 00:05:00 (preferably under a flame, inside a laminar-flow hood, or inverted on a piece of kitchen paper, to prevent contamination) to evaporate the remaining ethanol. Alternatively, the pellets can be dried under a filtered stream of N 2 , or in a speed-vac. The pellets might not be completely dry at this point, but the remaining liquid should be pure water.
Resuspend each pellet in 50 µL to 100 µL, Low-EDTA TE Buffer and combine both subsamples into one of the non-stick tubes.
Remove co-extracted humic substances using OneStep™ PCR Inhibitor Removal Kit This step is optional but very recommended. OneStep PCR Inhibitor Removal Kit Zymo Research Catalog #D6030
Resuspend the resin in the column by brief vortexing (if the column is dry, add 100 μl of RNAse-free water prior to vortexing.)
Loosen the cap by a quarter twist, place the column in a collection tube and centrifuge 8000 x g, Room temperature, 3 minutes
Place the column in a fresh 1.5 ml tube, pipette the entire TNA extract (up to 200 μl) on top of the resin. Be careful not to disturb the resin with the tip
Centrifuge 8000 x g, Room temperature, 1 minute
Discard the column and retain the extract in the tube.
Optional: load 5 µL to 10 µL of the total NA extract on an agarose gel to evaluate its quantity and quality using electrophoresis.
Quantify total NA extract using RiboGreen™ and PicoGreen™ (see Guidelines and Warnings ) or only DNA using PicoGreen™. Qant-iT™ PicoGreen ® dsDNA Quantification by Roey Angel

The extract can be used directly as a DNA template for downstream applications. We recommend aliquoting 10 µL to 100 µL as a work-template to be stored at -20 °C , in order to minimise freeze-thaw cycles. Genomic DNA Clean & Concentrator-10 Zymo Research Catalog #D4011
Depending on quality and quantity, aliquot 10 µL to 100 µL for RNA purification. Proceed to Purification of RNA from a DNA/RNA Extract. Purification of RNA from a DNA/RNA Extract by Roey Angel
If the removal of RNA is necessary, follow up with RNaseH treatment and purification using e.g. Genomic DNA Clean & Concentrator. Genomic DNA Clean & Concentrator-10 Zymo Research Catalog #D4011
